---
title: "Browser lab – the whole exercise, no installation"
subtitle: "Track A: everything runs in this tab"
engine: knitr
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
    webr:
      packages:
        - dplyr
        - readr
        - ggplot2
        - broom
        - fixest
        - plm
        - dagitty
resources:
  - ../data/rep_data.csv
  - ../data/teney_panel.csv
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

::: {.callout-important icon=false}
## Before you touch anything – the R engine is downloading

The R engine downloads into your browser the first time you open this page (10–30 seconds on conference wifi) – read Task A while it warms up. You will see the first code cell switch from a loading spinner to a "Run Code" button when it is ready.

Keep this tab open. Your work lives in this page until you refresh – there is nothing to install, nothing to save to disk, and no account needed to run the analysis. (You will only need a GitHub account at the very end, for Task E, if you want to publish.)
:::

```{webr}
#| edit: false
#| autorun: true
#| output: false
# Shared setup – this runs automatically once the engine is ready.
# It loads the two published artifacts into your browser session so every
# editable cell below can use `rep` (the OSF file) and `ten` (the corrected panel).
# The files arrive at data/... in the browser via the page's `resources:`.
rep <- read.csv("data/rep_data.csv")
ten <- read.csv("data/teney_panel.csv")
```

You are **analyst no. 6**. Five analysts reanalysed a claim from Teney (2016) for the Multi100 project; you are repeating the exercise with a transparent pipeline. The full task description, the claim, and the five-analyst table live on the [Exercise overview](index.qmd) – this page is the runnable version of Tasks A to D for people working entirely in the browser.

---

## Task A – Concepts (warm-up while the engine loads)

**Before touching data, fix the vocabulary.** Four reanalysts walk into a seminar. Three are doing one of our three R's; one is doing something else entirely. Read the four scenarios, then label each in the code cell below.

1. A reviewer reruns the authors' Stata `.do` file on the authors' data and gets a *different* coefficient from the one printed in the paper. (Is the activity still named by what they *tried* to do, even though it failed?)
2. Same data, but the analyst re-implements the model in R instead of Stata and centres the predictor. Same conclusion. (Careful: is changing the *software* the same as changing the *analysis*?)
3. A preregistered team tests the identical hypothesis on a *separate, much larger* dataset chosen to be theoretically equivalent, and gets results that contradict the original.
4. A different team tests the same underlying idea on social-media text using a machine-learning classifier – a method the original never used.

Replace each `______` with one of `"reproducibility"`, `"robustness"`, `"replicability"`, `"generalisability"` (keep the quotes). Press **Run Code**, then open the solution.

```{webr}
#| exercise: vocab
labels <- c(
  scenario_1 = ______,
  scenario_2 = ______,
  scenario_3 = ______,
  scenario_4 = ______
)
labels
```

::: {.solution exercise="vocab"}
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Answers – and the two traps

1. **reproducibility** – rerunning the same code on the same data. Here it *failed* to match, but the activity is still reproducibility; "not reproduced" is the verdict.
2. **reproducibility** (the grey area) – re-implementing on the same data in different software is a re-computation. Centring the predictor nudges toward **robustness**, so you can defend either – the point is to name your reason.
3. **replicability** – same question, independent new data chosen to be theoretically equivalent.
4. **generalisability** – the impostor: new data *and* a new method at once. It is the fourth cell of the matrix, not one of the three we test today.
:::
:::

So, which of the three are *you* about to do? Complete the sentence:

```{webr}
#| exercise: which_r
this_exercise_is <- ______   # "reproducibility" | "robustness" | "replicability"
the_data_we_use  <- ______   # "the same as" | "new, independent of"
our_analysis_is  <- ______   # "identical to" | "a defensible alternative to"

cat("This exercise is a", this_exercise_is, "check: we use",
    the_data_we_use, "the original study's data, and run an analysis that is",
    our_analysis_is, "the original.\n")
```

::: {.solution exercise="which_r"}
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Answer

A **robustness** check: **the same as** the original study's data, analysed in **a defensible alternative to** the original analysis. Same data, different defensible choices – that is exactly Task D2.
:::
:::

---

## Task B – Get the data and look at it

The published artifact `rep_data.csv` is already loaded for you as `rep`. It comes from the analyst's fork on OSF (osf.io/6zqct – "Multi100 | Teney_EurSocioRev_2016 | Analyst_C6HJR"); the official Multi100 archival record is at osf.io/8rtwe. Track B participants fetch it over the network with the `osfr` package; in the browser it is pre-loaded so you can get straight to the analysis.

Run the cell below to inspect the panel. Edit it freely – this is your environment.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# How many countries, how many years, how big is the panel?
dplyr::glimpse(rep)

cat("\nCountries:", dplyr::n_distinct(rep$cntry),
    " Years:", dplyr::n_distinct(rep$year),
    " Rows:", nrow(rep), "\n")
```

You should see **270 rows**, **27 countries**, **10 years** (2004–2013), and 11 columns. The outcome we care about first is `mcosmopolitan` – the mean 'cosmopolitan' framing of the EU in each country-year (a 0–1 scale; see the [codebook](../data/teney_panel_codebook.md)). The exposure is `unemp`, the annual unemployment rate.

Now look at the outcome over time, one small panel per country:

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 6
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(rep, aes(x = year, y = mcosmopolitan)) +
  geom_line(colour = "grey55") +
  geom_point(size = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ cntry, ncol = 6) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2004, 2008, 2013)) +
  labs(
    title = "Cosmopolitan EU framing by country, 2004–2013",
    x = NULL, y = "Mean cosmopolitan framing"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9)
```

Most countries drift gently; a few move sharply around the 2009 crisis. That variation – within a country, over time – is exactly what the fixed-effects model in Task D1 uses.

---

## Task C – Estimand and DAG

Before fitting anything, say what you are trying to estimate, in words. A template you can adapt:

> The **unit-specific quantity** is the change in a country-year's mean cosmopolitan EU framing associated with a one-point change in that country's unemployment rate. The **target population** is EU member states over 2004–2013. The **aggregation** is the average of those unit-specific effects, holding fixed each country's time-invariant traits and each year's EU-wide shocks.

That last clause is a causal claim, and a DAG makes it checkable. The cell below builds a directed acyclic graph with the **five edges we are willing to commit to** – the same starter DAG as in the template repository's `R/dag_starter.R`. Five candidate edges are commented out – uncomment the ones you believe in, re-run, and watch how `adjustmentSets()` changes.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
library(dagitty)

# The five edges we are willing to commit to:
committed_edges <- "
  unemployment -> framing
  country -> unemployment
  country -> framing
  year -> unemployment
  year -> framing
"

# Candidate edges – uncomment the ones you can defend, then re-run.
# (dagitty has no comment syntax of its own, so we add them here in R.)
candidate_edges <- c(
  # "growth -> unemployment",      # growth as a confounder of the exposure
  # "growth -> framing",           # ... and of the outcome
  # "unemployment -> bailout",     # bailout as a mediator ...
  # "bailout -> framing",          # ... on the path to framing
  # "politicisation -> framing"    # party cueing shapes framing directly
)

dag <- dagitty(paste0(
  "dag {", committed_edges, paste(candidate_edges, collapse = "\n"), "}"
))

exposures(dag) <- "unemployment"
outcomes(dag)  <- "framing"

# tidy left-to-right layout (coordinates for unused nodes are simply ignored)
coordinates(dag) <- list(
  x = c(unemployment = 0, framing = 3, growth = 1.5,
        country = 1.5, year = 2.4, bailout = 0.6, politicisation = 2.8),
  y = c(unemployment = 0, framing = 0, growth = 1.6,
        country = 2.2, year = -1.6, bailout = -2.2, politicisation = 1.8)
)

# We use dagitty's own base-graphics plot here, NOT ggdag. ggdag pulls in
# ggraph + igraph, which are too heavy for webR and can crash the browser
# session; the prettier ggdag version is in the template repo and on the
# slides. plot() is light and reliable in the browser.
tryCatch(plot(dag), error = function(e)
  cat("(DAG plot unavailable in this session – see the slides.",
      conditionMessage(e), ")\n"))

cat("\nMinimal sufficient adjustment set(s) to identify unemployment -> framing:\n")
print(adjustmentSets(dag, exposure = "unemployment", outcome = "framing"))
```

With the five committed edges, the adjustment set is `{ country, year }` – exactly what a two-way fixed-effects model absorbs. That is no accident: the DAG and the Task D1 model encode the same identification claim. Uncomment the `growth` edges and the set becomes `{ country, year, growth }` – now your model needs `+ growth` to match your beliefs. Write two sentences for your own notes: what do two-way fixed effects adjust for, and what can they **not** fix? (Hint: anything time-varying within a country that you have not measured.)

---

## Task D1 – Reproduce the constrained result

This is the **reproducibility** check. Fit the two-way fixed-effects model the Multi100 Task-2 constraints point to:

```
mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c   with fixed effects for country and year
```

where `unemp_c` is the unemployment rate centred on its grand mean. The target is **t = −3.853** (df = 233, N = 270) on the published `rep_data.csv`.

The cell below is an exercise. Replace the blanks (`______`) with the missing code, then press "Run Code". Stuck? Open the hint; want to check? Open the solution.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: reproduce
# make the data available inside the exercise (independent of the cells above)
rep <- read.csv("data/rep_data.csv")
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: reproduce
# 1. centre the unemployment rate on its grand mean
rep$unemp_c <- rep$unemp - ______

# 2. fit two-way fixed effects with fixest::feols
#    the part after the | adds the fixed effects
m1 <- feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | ______, data = rep)

broom::tidy(m1)
```

::: {.hint exercise="reproduce"}
::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint 1 – centring

To centre a variable on its grand mean, subtract the mean of the whole column:

```r
rep$unemp_c <- rep$unemp - mean(rep$unemp)
```

With fixed effects present this does not change the t-statistic, but it is the transformation the constrained specification asks for, so do it explicitly.
:::
:::

::: {.hint exercise="reproduce"}
::: {.callout-note collapse="false"}
## Hint 2 – the fixed-effects syntax

In `fixest::feols`, the bar `|` separates the regression from the fixed effects. To add country and year fixed effects, list them after the bar separated by `+`:

```r
feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = rep)
```
:::
:::

::: {.solution exercise="reproduce"}
::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Fully worked solution

```r
rep$unemp_c <- rep$unemp - mean(rep$unemp)
m1 <- feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = rep)
broom::tidy(m1)
```

You should get `estimate ≈ −0.00341`, `std.error ≈ 0.00088`, `statistic ≈ −3.85`. `feols` reports IID standard errors by default when two fixed-effect dimensions are present, which is why it matches the `plm` value to three decimals.
:::
:::

### The result you got is not the result on file

Here is the twist that makes this a workshop and not a button-press. The value recorded for this analyst (C6HJR) in the Multi100 dataset is **t = −3.804**, not the −3.853 you just produced. Same model, same published data, two different numbers. Run the next cell to see them side by side:

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
rep$unemp_c <- rep$unemp - mean(rep$unemp)
m_rep <- feols(mcosmopolitan ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = rep)

ten$unemp_c <- ten$unemp - mean(ten$unemp)
m_ten <- feols(mcosmo ~ unemp_c | cntry + year, data = ten)

cat(sprintf("published rep_data.csv : t = %.3f\n", broom::tidy(m_rep)$statistic))
cat(sprintf("corrected panel        : t = %.3f  <- the recorded value\n",
            broom::tidy(m_ten)$statistic))
```

::: {.callout-important collapse="false"}
## Why the discrepancy?

The result recorded for this analyst (C6HJR) in the Multi100 dataset is **t = −3.804**, not −3.853. Why is that?

The original data-prep script read the EB 70.1 file (ZA4819) twice by mistake: once for 2008 (correct) and again in place of EB 72.4 (ZA4994) for 2009 (incorrect). **The analyst spotted this during the project and corrected it.** The recorded result came from the corrected pipeline. The annotation is preserved in the analyst's fork (`osf.io/6zqct`, folder `moreh_rep_vAPR2025/`): the script carries the note "APRIL 2025 – Mistake in dataset here; the dataset associated with 'eb724' should be 'ZA4994_v3-0-0.dta'". The official Multi100 component (`osf.io/8rtwe`) preserved only the earlier, uncorrected folder; the fork publishes the full trail.

Coding can go wrong - even during a reanalysis! Keeping materials open and well documented helps spot any mistakes and correct them. That's the purpose of reproducibility.
:::

---

## Task D2 – Your fork in the path

This is the **robustness** check, and it is the part your classmates cannot copy from you. You pick **one** deviation from the published specification, justify it in two sentences, and report what it does to the estimate. Together the room samples the multiverse of defensible analyses.

The cell below has six choices at the top – the same axes as the full [specification menu](spec-menu.qmd). Change one (or more), read the comments for the allowed options, and run it. A small helper fits whichever specification you describe.

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
# ---- YOUR CHOICES (edit these) -------------------------------------------
my_outcome       <- "mcosmo"     # mcosmo | mutil | mcomm | mlib | mpos | mneg
my_exposure_form <- "centred"    # raw | centred | log | logz
my_control       <- "none"       # none | growth
my_estimator     <- "feols_tw"   # feols_tw | plm_tw | plm_re | ols_cluster
my_sample        <- "all"        # all | precrisis | crisis | nobailout | no_gr_es
my_weights       <- "none"       # none | n_cy
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------

# ---- helper: fit the specification you described above -------------------
d <- ten   # the corrected panel from the shared setup
d <- switch(my_sample,
  all       = d,
  precrisis = subset(d, year <= 2008),
  crisis    = subset(d, year >= 2009),
  nobailout = subset(d, bailout == 0),
  no_gr_es  = subset(d, !(cntry %in% c("GR", "ES"))))

d$expo <- switch(my_exposure_form,
  raw     = d$unemp,
  centred = d$unemp - mean(d$unemp),
  log     = log(d$unemp),
  logz    = as.numeric(scale(log(d$unemp))))

d$y  <- d[[my_outcome]]
wts  <- if (my_weights == "n_cy") d$n_cy else NULL
rhs  <- if (my_control == "growth") "expo + growth" else "expo"

my_model <- switch(my_estimator,
  feols_tw    = feols(as.formula(paste("y ~", rhs, "| cntry + year")),
                      data = d, weights = wts),
  plm_tw      = plm(as.formula(paste("y ~", rhs)), data = d,
                    index = c("cntry", "year"), effect = "twoways", model = "within"),
  plm_re      = plm(as.formula(paste("y ~", rhs)), data = d,
                    index = c("cntry", "year"), model = "random"),
  ols_cluster = feols(as.formula(paste("y ~", rhs)), data = d,
                      weights = wts, cluster = ~ cntry))

summary(my_model)
```

### Preregister, then report

The point of preregistration is that you commit to a choice **before** you see what it does. It stops you quietly picking the specification that happens to give the answer you wanted.

::: {.callout-warning icon=false}
## Do this in order

1. **Preregister first.** Before you run your chosen specification, write it down where you cannot change it unseen: type your spec and a one-sentence reason into the cell below as a comment, and tell your partner. That is your commitment. (Track B participants do the same thing with a `prereg:` git commit – the commit timestamp is the record.)
2. **Then run** your specification.
3. **Then report** – run `report_result()` below and click the submission link it prints.
:::

Paste the function below into the editable cell (or run it here after running your model) to get your one-click submission link. It is self-contained and works for `fixest`, `plm`, and `lm` models:

```{webr}
#| autorun: false
report_result <- function(model, spec = "unspecified", term = NULL) {
  # pull the coefficient table in a model-agnostic way
  if (inherits(model, "fixest")) {
    ct <- summary(model)$coeftable
  } else if (inherits(model, "plm")) {
    ct <- summary(model)$coefficients
  } else if (inherits(model, "lm")) {
    ct <- coef(summary(model))
  } else {
    stop("Unsupported model type: ", paste(class(model), collapse = "/"))
  }
  rn <- rownames(ct)
  # the exposure is the first coefficient that is not an intercept
  if (is.null(term)) term <- rn[!grepl("Intercept", rn)][1]

  b <- ct[term, 1]; se <- ct[term, 2]; tval <- ct[term, 3]

  if (inherits(model, "fixest")) {
    dfree <- as.integer(fixest::degrees_freedom(model, type = "t"))
    nobs_ <- as.integer(model$nobs)
  } else if (inherits(model, "plm")) {
    dfree <- as.integer(model$df.residual)
    nobs_ <- as.integer(nrow(model$model))
  } else {
    dfree <- as.integer(summary(model)$df[2])
    nobs_ <- as.integer(length(model$residuals))
  }

  r <- tval / sqrt(tval^2 + dfree)   # partial correlation, as Multi100 used

  # ---- One-click submission to the class-results Google Form ----------------
  # The Form turns your numbers into a dot on the live Multiverse chart. Fill in
  # FORM_ID and the entry.* field codes once the Form exists (the facilitator
  # does this; see facilitator/live-results-setup.md, step 1). Until then the
  # printed link is a placeholder, but the printed summary always works.
  FORM_ID <- "1FAIpQLSezoAEOnZUfP4pkfN28_XtEBwSyc2RLsXB-h8RPSegF7_t76A"
  field <- c(spec = "entry.832496914", b = "entry.1656311800", se = "entry.1337458262",
             t = "entry.1940779437", df = "entry.1682711955", n = "entry.1586392724",
             r = "entry.1660273001")
  value <- c(spec = spec, b = sprintf("%.5f", b), se = sprintf("%.5f", se),
             t = sprintf("%.3f", tval), df = as.character(dfree),
             n = as.character(nobs_), r = sprintf("%.3f", r))
  query <- paste(vapply(names(field), function(k)
    paste0(field[[k]], "=", utils::URLencode(value[[k]], reserved = TRUE)),
    character(1)), collapse = "&")
  url <- sprintf(
    "https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/%s/viewform?usp=pp_url&%s", FORM_ID, query)

  cat(sprintf(paste0("Your result:  spec = %s\n",
              "  b = %.5f | se = %.5f | t = %.3f | df = %d | n = %d | r = %.3f\n\n"),
              spec, b, se, tval, dfree, nobs_, r))
  cat("Submit (opens the form with your numbers pre-filled - just press Send):\n")
  cat(url, "\n")
  invisible(list(term = term, b = b, se = se, t = tval, df = dfree,
                 n = nobs_, r = r, url = url))
}

# example on the model you fitted above:
report_result(my_model, spec = "mcosmo ~ unemp_c | feols_tw | all")
```

Click the submission link it prints and press Send. Your result appears as an orange dot on the live [Multiverse]({{< var live-results-url >}}) chart within seconds. That is your contribution to the class multiverse.

---

## Task E – Still want the GitHub experience?

You did the whole analysis without installing anything – but Track A participants can still finish with a published repository. Three routes, easiest first:

1. **Save your code.** Copy the cells you edited into a plain text file on your laptop (call it `analysis.R` or `report.qmd`). Everything you need is in this tab.
2. **Make a repo by upload.** On [github.com](https://github.com), create a new public repository, then use **Add file → Upload files** to drag your saved file in. No git, no command line – your first repo in about three minutes.
3. **Edit in the browser.** On any GitHub repository, press the `.` key to open **github.dev** (a full VS Code editor in the browser), or change `github.com` to `github.dev` in the URL. You can edit, commit, and push entirely online.

The [Cheatsheet](cheatsheet.qmd) has the click-by-click version, including how to switch on GitHub Pages so your report gets a live URL.

---

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## After the workshop – the full pipeline

Everything you did here also exists as a proper reproducible project: a template repository you clone, edit in Positron, and publish. If you want the version with version control, `osfr` data-fetching, and automated rendering, start from the [Full pipeline track](index.qmd) – the [`replication-lab` template](https://github.com/CodeMoreh/applied-replication) is yours to reuse.
:::
